region_of_origin: Western / International; widely consumed in Singapore

serving.description: 1 sandwich (2 slices white bread with tuna mayo filling)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one sandwich made with two slices of white bread and a standard tuna mayonnaise filling, weighing approximately 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna Mayo Sandwich — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Tuna Mayo Sandwich (cr_id F05020116).

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread reference; Atkinson FS et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a tuna mayo sandwich as a composite food. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being white bread (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database). The tuna and mayonnaise filling contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the composite. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 27.97 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 29.4 for a 150 g serving (41.96 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 463 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this sandwich due to its high sodium content (463 mg/100 g), primarily from canned tuna, mayonnaise, and white bread; substituting low-sodium canned tuna and reduced-sodium bread can meaningfully reduce the sodium load per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~70) with 27.97 g carbohydrate and only 2.02 g dietary fibre per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or minimise this sandwich in its standard form because the white bread base has a high glycaemic index (~70) and the low fibre content (2.02 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering of postprandial glucose; switching to wholemeal or multigrain bread and adding high-fibre vegetables would substantially improve glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (463 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate load; moderate fat (6.04 g/100 g) with tuna-derived omega-3 as a partial mitigating factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content and refined carbohydrate base are unfavourable for blood pressure and lipid management; however, the tuna filling provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, so choosing low-sodium canned tuna, wholemeal bread, and limiting portion size to one sandwich per meal is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white bread drives pro-inflammatory glycaemic load; high sodium may exacerbate tissue inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace white bread with wholemeal bread to reduce the pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate load, use low-sodium tuna to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor response to any nightshade ingredients (e.g., tomato) that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna mayo sandwich is a widely consumed Western-style sandwich popular in Singapore, particularly as a school canteen and breakfast item. It consists of two slices of white bread filled with a mixture of canned tuna and mayonnaise, sometimes supplemented with vegetables such as cucumber or lettuce. The texture is soft from the white bread and creamy from the mayonnaise-bound tuna filling. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 207 kcal, 10.93 g protein, 6.04 g fat, and 27.97 g carbohydrate with 2.02 g dietary fibre. The sodium content is high at 463 mg/100 g, driven by canned tuna, mayonnaise, and commercial white bread. The glycaemic index is approximately 70 (high), reflecting the white bread base, making this sandwich a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The tuna filling provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, which are cardioprotective, but the refined carbohydrate base and sodium level are the primary clinical concerns. Substituting wholemeal or multigrain bread and using low-sodium canned tuna would substantially improve the nutritional profile. Calcium is notably present at 121 mg/100 g from the bread, and iron at 3.83 mg/100 g. Known in Singapore as 金枪鱼蛋黄酱三明治 (Chinese), Sandwic tuna mayo (Malay), டுனா மயோ சாண்ட்விச் (Tamil), and ट्यूना मेयो सैंडविच (Hindi).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tuna sandwich?
Is tuna sandwich good for diabetics?
Can I eat tuna sandwich if I have high blood pressure?
Tuna mayo sandwich nutrition facts Singapore
Is tuna sandwich suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB Foodo provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed and enrichment_source manual. All per_100g values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact food item (cr_id F05020116). GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite sandwich food as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 1.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for a comparable tuna salad sandwich (USDA SR Legacy item 21116) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is an adapted value and should be verified against the HPB source when detailed fatty acid data become available. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite GI measurement for tuna mayo sandwich was found in approved sources. Halal status assessed as likely halal given tuna and standard mayonnaise ingredients, but consumers should verify the specific brand of mayonnaise used. Although this food is of Western origin, it is widely consumed in Singapore across all communities; local-language names have been provided in Chinese (金枪鱼蛋黄酱三明治 / 鮪魚美乃滋三明治), Malay (Sandwic tuna mayo), Tamil (டுனா மயோ சாண்ட்விச்), and Hindi (ट्यूना मेयो सैंडविच) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore-consumed foods.
